questions.question:
Consider the grammar shown below.

S -> CC
C -> cC | d

This grammar is

A. LL(1)
B. SLR(1) but not LL(1)
C. LALR(1) but not SLR(1)
D. LR(1) but not LALR(1)
questions.to_find: Which parsing class does the grammar S->CC, C->cC|d belong to?

understand.plain: We are given a grammar with two productions for C. We need to determine the strongest (highest-level) parsing class this grammar belongs to. LL(1) is the strongest — if a grammar is LL(1), it is also SLR(1) and LALR(1) and LR(1). We check whether the grammar is LL(1) by computing FIRST and FOLLOW sets and checking for conflicts.

keywords.explain: A grammar is LL(1) if for every non-terminal A with productions A -> alpha | beta, FIRST(alpha) and FIRST(beta) are disjoint, and if epsilon is in FIRST(alpha) then FIRST(beta) and FOLLOW(A) are disjoint.
keywords.example: A -> aB | bC is LL(1) if 'a' and 'b' are distinct.

keywords.explain: FIRST(alpha) is the set of terminals that can begin a string derived from alpha.
FOLLOW(A) is the set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of A in some sentential form.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='440' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='440' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Grammar: S -> CC, C -> cC | d</text><text x='20' y='70' class='sub ink'>FIRST(C -> cC) = {c}</text><text x='20' y='95' class='sub ink'>FIRST(C -> d)  = {d}</text><text x='20' y='120' class='sub ink'>FIRST sets are disjoint => no LL(1) conflict for C</text><text x='20' y='155' class='sub ink'>FIRST(S) = {c, d}, FOLLOW(S) = {$}</text><text x='20' y='180' class='sub ok'>Conclusion: Grammar is LL(1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing FIRST sets for C's productions: FIRST(cC)={c} and FIRST(d)={d} are disjoint, confirming LL(1).

given.aim: Classify the grammar S->CC, C->cC|d into LL(1), SLR(1), LALR(1) or LR(1) only.

terms.meaning: Start symbol S derives exactly two C's.
terms.example: String 'cdcd' can be derived: S => CC => cCd => cdcd (using C->cC then C->d for each C).
terms.connects: Determines the overall structure; S always derives exactly one string of the form (c^n d)(c^m d).

terms.meaning: C can be a c followed by another C, or just d.
terms.connects: The two alternatives for C have FIRST sets {c} and {d} which are disjoint — key to LL(1) check.

terms.meaning: LL(1) subset of LALR(1) subset of LR(1). If a grammar is LL(1), it is in all classes.
terms.example: Proving LL(1) is the strongest claim.
terms.connects: If LL(1) is satisfied, options B, C, D are all weaker than A, so A is the correct answer.

given.plan: Compute FIRST sets for each alternative of every non-terminal. Check if they are disjoint (LL(1) condition). If so, the grammar is LL(1), which automatically makes it SLR(1) and LALR(1) as well. Report the strongest class.

steps.title: Compute FIRST sets
steps.formula_raw: FIRST(alpha) = set of terminals starting strings derivable from alpha
steps.note: Disjoint FIRST sets for all alternatives of every non-terminal is the core LL(1) condition (when no production derives epsilon).

steps.title: Verify LL(1) condition and classify
steps.formula_raw: LL(1): for each A -> alpha | beta, FIRST(alpha) ∩ FIRST(beta) = empty
steps.apply:
S has only one production (no conflict).
C -> cC | d: FIRST(cC) ∩ FIRST(d) = {c} ∩ {d} = empty — no conflict.
No production derives epsilon, so the FOLLOW-based condition is not triggered.
Hence the grammar is LL(1).
Since LL(1) => LALR(1) => SLR(1) => LR(1), option A is the strongest and correct answer.
steps.note: The grammar is deterministic and predictable with 1 lookahead — a parser can always decide which production to use just by looking at the next input symbol.

solution.result: The grammar is LL(1). Answer: A. LL(1)

questions.formula_note: LL(1) membership check via disjoint FIRST sets for all alternatives of each non-terminal.

questions.question:
Consider the translation scheme shown below.

S  -> TR
R  -> +T { print('+'); } R | epsilon
T  -> num { print(num.val); }

Here num is a token that represents an integer and num.val represents the corresponding integer value. For an input string '9+5+2', this translation scheme will print

A. 9+5+2
B. 9 5 + 2 +
C. 9 5 2 + +
D. + + 9 5 2
questions.to_find: Output printed when the translation scheme is applied to input '9+5+2'.

understand.plain: This is an SDT (Syntax-Directed Translation) scheme. The actions (print statements) are embedded inline in the grammar rules. When parsing '9+5+2', the actions fire in the order the parser encounters them during a leftmost derivation (top-down parse). We trace the parse tree and note when each action fires.

keywords.explain: A grammar with semantic actions embedded at positions within production bodies. Actions fire when the parser reaches that position in the rule.
keywords.example: T -> num { print(num.val); } fires print immediately after matching the num token.

keywords.explain: In a top-down parse, an action at the end of a production body fires after all symbols to its left in the body have been processed.
keywords.example: R -> +T { print('+'); } R fires print('+') after matching '+' and T but before R is expanded.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Parse of 9 + 5 + 2</text><text x='20' y='60' class='sub ink'>S => T R</text><text x='20' y='85' class='sub ink'>  T => num(9) { print(9) }   -- prints: 9</text><text x='20' y='110' class='sub ink'>  R => +T { print(+) } R</text><text x='20' y='135' class='sub ink'>    T => num(5) { print(5) }  -- prints: 5</text><text x='20' y='160' class='sub ink'>    { print(+) }             -- prints: +</text><text x='20' y='185' class='sub ink'>    R => +T { print(+) } R</text><text x='20' y='210' class='sub ink'>      T => num(2) { print(2) } -- prints: 2</text><text x='20' y='235' class='sub ok'>      { print(+) }            -- prints: +</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Parse tree trace for 9+5+2 showing order of action execution: print(9), print(5), print(+), print(2), print(+) yielding '9 5 + 2 +'.

given.aim: Trace the translation scheme on input '9+5+2' and determine the printed output.

terms.meaning: S expands to T (a number) followed by R (the rest: additions).
terms.connects: Sets up the parse; T fires first printing the first number.

terms.meaning: R matches a '+', then T (a number which prints itself), then fires print('+'), then recurses into R.
terms.example: First R matches '+5', T prints '5', then print('+') fires.
terms.connects: The + is printed AFTER the number T to its left — this is a postfix-like ordering.

terms.meaning: T matches a number token and immediately prints its value.
terms.connects: All numbers are printed before their operators because num.val print comes before the { print('+') } action in R.

given.plan: Simulate a top-down leftmost parse of '9+5+2' and fire each action in order. Track the printed sequence.

steps.title: Expand S and match first number
steps.apply:
S -> T R
T -> num { print(num.val) }
Match num=9, fire print(9).
Output so far: 9
steps.note: Numbers always print before their operator because T -> num {print} fires first in the R production.

steps.title: Expand first R (matches +5)
steps.apply:
Match '+', then expand T -> num { print(num.val) } with num=5.
Fire print(5). Output so far: 9 5
Then fire print('+'). Output so far: 9 5 +
Now recurse into R.
steps.note: The print('+') action comes after T in the body, so the number 5 is printed before '+'.

steps.title: Expand second R (matches +2)
steps.apply:
Match '+', then expand T -> num { print(num.val) } with num=2.
Fire print(2). Output so far: 9 5 + 2
Then fire print('+'). Output so far: 9 5 + 2 +
R -> epsilon, done.
steps.note: Final output is 9 5 + 2 + which matches option C.

solution.result: The translation scheme prints: 9 5 + 2 +. Answer: B. 9 5 + 2 +

questions.formula_note: Inline SDT action ordering during top-down parse: actions fire left-to-right as their surrounding symbols are matched.

questions.to_find: E.value for the expression 2 # 3 & 5 # 6 & 4 using the given translation rules.

understand.plain: The grammar defines two operators: # (which acts like multiplication) and & (which acts like addition). The grammar structure tells us that # has lower precedence than & (since E uses # and E uses T as operand, while T uses &). Both operators are left-associative. We parse the expression and compute the value bottom-up.

keywords.explain: In a grammar, the operator at a lower level (closer to the start symbol) has lower precedence. Here E uses # and T uses &, so # has lower precedence (binds less tightly) than &.
Both E -> E1 # T and T -> T1 & F have the recursive symbol on the left (E1, T1), so both operators associate left-to-right.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Expression: 2 # 3 & 5 # 6 & 4</text><text x='20' y='60' class='sub ink'>Precedence: & > #  (& binds tighter)</text><text x='20' y='85' class='sub ink'>Left-assoc: (2 # (3&5)) # (6&4)</text><text x='20' y='115' class='sub ink'>Step 1: T1 = 3 & 5 = 3+5 = 8</text><text x='20' y='140' class='sub ink'>Step 2: T2 = 6 & 4 = 6+4 = 10</text><text x='20' y='165' class='sub ink'>Step 3: E1 = 2 # T1 = 2*8 = 16</text><text x='20' y='195' class='sub ok'>Step 4: E  = E1 # T2 = 16*10 = 160... wait recalculate</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Parsing 2#3&5#6&4 with & as addition (+) and # as multiplication (*). Left-associativity of # applied: ((2#(3&5))#(6&4)) = (2*8)*(6+4) = 16*10 = 160.

given.aim: Evaluate 2 # 3 & 5 # 6 & 4 using the grammar's operator semantics (# = multiply, & = add, & has higher precedence, both left-associative).

terms.explain: Defined at E level: E.value = E1.value * T.value. So # computes multiplication.
terms.connects: Lower precedence than &, so & groups first.

terms.explain: Defined at T level: T.value = T1.value + F.value. So & computes addition.
terms.connects: Higher precedence than #, so & groups before #.

terms.meaning: Both # and & are left-associative due to left-recursive grammar rules.
terms.connects: Determines how consecutive same-operator expressions group.

given.plan: Parse the expression respecting precedence (& before #) and left-associativity. Compute & sub-expressions first, then #.

steps.title: Identify structure using precedence and associativity
steps.formula_raw: & has higher precedence than #; both are left-associative
steps.apply:
Expression: 2 # 3 & 5 # 6 & 4
With # as E-level (lower precedence) and & as T-level (higher precedence):
The expression is parsed as a sequence of E-level productions.
Breaking by # (left-associative): ((2) # (3 & 5)) # (6 & 4)
First, compute the T-level sub-expressions grouped by &.
T1: 3 & 5 = 3 + 5 = 8
T2: 6 & 4 = 6 + 4 = 10
steps.note: & groups tighter, so 3 & 5 and 6 & 4 are computed first.

steps.title: Compute # expressions left-to-right
steps.apply:
E1 = 2 # T1 = 2 # 8 = 2 * 8 = 16
E  = E1 # T2 = 16 # 10 = 16 * 10 = 160

However, checking if 2 is itself a T (single num): F=2, T=2, E=2.
So: E = (2 # (3&5)) # (6&4) = (2*8) * (6+4) = 16 * 10 = 160.

Wait — checking option A=200: perhaps 2 # 3 is one group: T for 3&5? Let me re-examine.
Actually with left-assoc on #: the whole expression with two # operators:
E -> E1 # T gives: first E1=2, T=(3&5)=8, so E_partial = 2*8=16.
Then E_partial # T_2 where T_2=(6&4)=10: E_final = 16*10 = 160.
Option C = 160.
steps.note: The computation yields 160, which is option C. The correct answer is C. 160.

solution.result: E.value = 160. Answer: C. 160 (options A=200, B=180, C=160, D=40)

questions.formula_note: Operator precedence from grammar hierarchy (lower in grammar = lower precedence) and left-recursive grammar giving left-associativity.

questions.question:
Which of the following grammar rules violate the requirements of an operator grammar? P, Q, R, S are non-terminals, and a, b are terminals.

I.   P -> QR
II.  P -> aQbR
III. P -> epsilon
IV.  P -> a Q b R a

A. (I) only
B. (I) and (III) only
C. (II) and (III) only
D. (III) and (IV) only
questions.to_find: Which grammar rules violate the definition of an operator grammar?

understand.plain: An operator grammar is a special type of grammar used in operator-precedence parsing. It has two restrictions: (1) no production has two adjacent non-terminals on the right-hand side, and (2) no production has epsilon (empty string) on the right-hand side. We check each rule against these two conditions.

keywords.explain: A grammar where (1) no right-hand side has two adjacent non-terminals, and (2) no right-hand side is epsilon.
keywords.example: P -> aQb is valid (Q is not adjacent to another NT). P -> QR is invalid (Q and R are adjacent NTs).

keywords.explain: Two non-terminals appearing next to each other in a production body (with no terminal between them).
keywords.example: In P -> QR, Q and R are adjacent NTs — this violates operator grammar.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='240'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='460' height='240' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Operator Grammar Checks</text><text x='20' y='65' class='sub ink'>I.   P -> QR       : QR adjacent NTs</text><text x='180' y='65' class='sub no'>VIOLATES</text><text x='20' y='95' class='sub ink'>II.  P -> aQbR     : no adjacent NTs</text><text x='230' y='95' class='sub ok'>VALID</text><text x='20' y='125' class='sub ink'>III. P -> epsilon   : empty production</text><text x='230' y='125' class='sub no'>VIOLATES</text><text x='20' y='155' class='sub ink'>IV.  P -> aQbRa    : no adjacent NTs</text><text x='230' y='155' class='sub ok'>VALID</text><text x='20' y='195' class='lbl ink'>Answer: (I) and (III) only</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table checking each rule: Rule I (P->QR) violates because Q and R are adjacent non-terminals; Rule III (P->epsilon) violates because epsilon productions are not allowed; Rules II and IV are valid.

given.aim: Identify which of the four given productions violate the operator grammar conditions.

terms.meaning: In any production body, you cannot have two non-terminals side by side (without a terminal between them).
terms.example: P -> Q R is invalid; P -> Q a R is valid.
terms.connects: Used to check rules I, II, IV.

terms.meaning: No production can have an empty (epsilon) right-hand side.
terms.example: P -> epsilon is not allowed in an operator grammar.

terms.meaning: Two non-terminals QR with no terminal between them.
terms.example: Q and R are adjacent — this violates condition 1.

given.plan: Check each of the four rules against the two operator grammar conditions: (1) no adjacent NTs, (2) no epsilon. List the violating rules.

steps.title: Check each rule for adjacent non-terminals
steps.formula_raw: Operator grammar: no two NTs adjacent in any RHS
steps.apply:
Rule I:   P -> QR    — Q and R are adjacent non-terminals (no terminal between them). VIOLATES.
Rule II:  P -> aQbR  — Q is flanked by 'a' and 'b'; R is at the end (rightmost). Between Q and b: terminal. Between b and R: terminal. No two NTs are adjacent. VALID.
Rule III: P -> epsilon — epsilon production. Does not have adjacent NTs, but check condition 2.
Rule IV:  P -> aQbRa — Q is between 'a' and 'b'; R is between 'b' and 'a'. No adjacent NTs. VALID.
steps.note: Only Rule I has adjacent NTs (QR with no terminal between them).

steps.title: Check for epsilon productions
steps.apply:
Rule III: P -> epsilon — this is an epsilon production. VIOLATES.
Rules I, II, IV have non-empty RHS.
steps.note: Epsilon productions are explicitly forbidden in operator grammars because they complicate the operator-precedence matrix.

steps.title: Compile the violating rules
steps.apply:
Violating rules: I (adjacent NTs) and III (epsilon production).
This matches option B: (I) and (III) only.
steps.note: Rules II and IV are valid operator grammar rules.

solution.result: Rules I and III violate operator grammar requirements. Answer: B. (I) and (III) only

questions.formula_note: Operator grammar definition: no two adjacent non-terminals on any RHS, and no epsilon productions.
